{{Short description|American geneticist (born 1954)}}

* Member, [[National Academy of Medicine]] (2019)}}
| website          = {{URL|http://wi.mit.edu/people/faculty/young}}

'''Richard Allen Young''' (born March 12, 1954) is an American [[geneticist]], a Member of [[Whitehead Institute]], and a professor of biology at the [[MIT Department of Biology|Massachusetts Institute of Technology]].<ref name=young_nas>{{cite news|title=Whitehead Member Richard Young elected to National Academy of Sciences|url=http://wi.mit.edu/news/archive/2012/whitehead-member-richard-young-elected-national-academy-sciences|access-date=15 October 2015|agency=Whitehead Institute News Office|date=May 1, 2012}}</ref> He is a pioneer in the [[systems biology]] of gene control who has developed genomics technologies and concepts key to understanding [[Regulation of gene expression|gene control]] in human health and disease. He has served as an advisor to the [[World Health Organization]] and the [[National Institutes of Health]].<ref name="young_nas" /> He is a member of the [[National Academy of Sciences]]<ref name="young_nas" /> and the [[National Academy of Medicine]].<ref>{{cite news |title=Whitehead Institute Member Richard Young elected to National Academy of Medicine |url=http://wi.mit.edu/news/archive/2019/whitehead-institute-member-richard-young-elected-national-academy-medicine |date=October 21, 2019}}</ref> [[Scientific American]] has recognized him as one of the top 50 leaders in science, technology and business.<ref>{{cite news|title=Scientific American 50: SA 50 Winners and Contributors|url=http://www.scientificamerican.com/article/scientific-american-50-sa-2006-12/|access-date=15 October 2015|work=Scientific American|date=November 12, 2006}}</ref> Young is among the most [https://recognition.webofsciencegroup.com/awards/highly-cited/2019/ Highly Cited Researchers] in his field.<ref>{{cite web |title=Highly Cited Researchers |url=https://recognition.webofsciencegroup.com/awards/highly-cited/2019/ |website=Web of Science Group}}</ref>

==Education==
Young was educated at [[Indiana University]] ([[Bachelor of Science]], 1975) and [[Yale University]] (PhD, 1979).<ref name=phd>{{cite thesis |degree=PhD |first=Richard Allen|last=Young |title=Regulatory signals in ribosomal RNA operons of Escherichia coli |publisher=Yale University  |date=1979 |oclc=638423416 |hdl=10079/bibid/9851541|id={{ProQuest|303012111}}}}</ref>

==Research and career==
Young has made major contributions to the understanding of gene control in human development and disease. He discovered that a small set of human [[embryonic stem cell]] master [[transcription factors]] form a core regulatory circuitry that controls the gene expression program of these cells.<ref name=boyer2005>{{cite journal|last1=Boyer|first1=LA|last2=Lee|first2=TI|last3=Cole|first3=MF|last4=Johnstone|first4=SE|last5=Levine|first5=SS|last6=Zucker|first6=JP|last7=Guenther|first7=MG|last8=Kumar|first8=RM|last9=Murray|first9=HL|last10=Jenner|first10=RG|last11=Gifford|first11=DK|last12=Melton|first12=DA|last13=Jaenisch|first13=R|last14=Young|first14=RA|title=Core transcriptional regulatory circuitry in human embryonic stem cells.|journal=Cell|date=23 September 2005|volume=122|issue=6|pages=947–56|pmid=16153702|doi=10.1016/j.cell.2005.08.020|pmc=3006442}}</ref> This concept of core regulatory circuitry helps guide current efforts to understand gene control, to develop reprogramming protocols for other human cell types and to understand how gene dysregulation contributes to disease.<ref>{{Cite journal|title = Transcriptional regulation and its misregulation in disease|last1 = Lee|first1 = TI|date = 2013|journal = Cell|doi = 10.1016/j.cell.2013.02.014|pmid = 23498934|last2 = Young|first2 = RA|issue = 6|pages = 1237–1251|pmc=3640494|volume=152}}</ref>

Young has introduced the concept of [[transcriptional amplification]] and described how much of the gene control program in cancer cells is amplified by oncogenic transcription factors such as c-MYC.<ref>{{Cite journal|title = Transcriptional amplification in tumor cells with elevated c-Myc|last1 = Lin|first1 = CY|date = 28 September 2012|journal = Cell|doi = 10.1016/j.cell.2012.08.026|pmid = 23021215|last2 = Lovén|first2 = J|last3 = Rahl|first3 = PB|last4 = Paranal|first4 = RM|last5 = Burge|first6 = JE|first5 = CB|last6 = Bradner|last7 = Lee|first7 = TI|last8 = Young|first8 = RA|issue = 1|volume = 151|pages = 56–67|pmc=3462372}}</ref> According to Young, most genes experience transcription initiation,<ref>{{cite journal|last1 = Guenther|first1 = MG|last2 = Levine|first2 = SS|last3 = Boyer|first3 = LA|last4 = Jaenisch|first4 = R|last5 = Young|first5 = RA|title = A chromatin landmark and transcription initiation at most promoters in human cells.|journal = Cell|date = 13 July 2007|volume = 130|issue = 1|pages = 77–88|pmid = 17632057|doi = 10.1016/j.cell.2007.05.042|pmc=3200295}}</ref> but it is the control of transcription elongation that plays key roles in cell control in health and disease.<ref>{{cite journal|last1 = Rahl|first1 = PB|last2 = Lin|first2 = CY|last3 = Seila|first3 = AC|last4 = Flynn|first4 = RA|last5 = McCuine|first5 = S|last6 = Burge|first6 = CB|last7 = Sharp|first7 = PA|last8 = Young|first8 = RA|title = c-Myc regulates transcriptional pause release.|journal = Cell|date = 30 April 2010|volume = 141|issue = 3|pages = 432–45|pmid = 20434984|doi = 10.1016/j.cell.2010.03.030|pmc=2864022}}</ref>

Young discovered that large clusters of gene control elements, called [[super-enhancer]]s, regulate genes that play prominent roles in cell identity.<ref>{{cite journal|last1 = Whyte|first1 = WA|last2 = Orlando|first2 = DA|last3 = Hnisz|first3 = D|last4 = Abraham|first4 = BJ|last5 = Lin|first5 = CY|last6 = Kagey|first6 = MH|last7 = Rahl|first7 = PB|last8 = Lee|first8 = TI|last9 = Young|first9 = RA|title = Master transcription factors and mediator establish super-enhancers at key cell identity genes.|journal = Cell|date = 11 April 2013|volume = 153|issue = 2|pages = 307–19|pmid = 23582322|doi = 10.1016/j.cell.2013.03.035|pmc=3653129}}</ref>  Furthermore, Young showed that disease-associated human genome variation occurs frequently in these super-enhancers<ref>{{Cite journal|title = Super-enhancers in the control of cell identity and disease|last1 = Hnisz|first1 = D|date = 2013|journal = Cell|doi = 10.1016/j.cell.2013.09.053|pmid = 24119843|last3 = Lau|first3 = A|last4 = Saint-André|first4 = V|last5 = Sigova|first5 = AA|last6 = Hoke|first6 = HA|last7 = Lee|first7 = TI|last8 = Young|last2 = Abraham|first2 = BJ|first8 = RA|issue = 4|pages = 934–947|pmc = 3841062|volume=155}}</ref> and that cancer cell super-enhancers are especially vulnerable to certain transcriptional drugs.<ref>{{Cite journal|title = Selective inhibition of tumor oncogenes by disruption of super-enhancers|last1 = Lovén|first1 = J|date = 2013|journal = Cell|doi = 10.1016/j.cell.2013.03.036|pmid = 23582323|last2 = Hoke|first2 = HA|last3 = Lin|first3 = CY|last4 = Lau|first6 = CR|first5 = DA|first9 = RA|first4 = A|last5 = Orlando|last6 = Vakoc|last7 = Bradner|first7 = JE|last8 = Lee|first8 = TI|last9 = Young|volume = 153|issue = 2|pages = 320–334|pmc = 3760967}}</ref>

Young has proposed that control of gene expression occurs within [[insulated neighborhood]]s, which are structural DNA loops that contain enhancers and their target genes.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Dowen|first1=JM|last2=Fan|first2=ZP|last3=Hnisz|first3=D|last4=Ren|first4=G|last5=Abraham|first5=BJ|last6=Zhang|first6=LN|last7=Weintraub|first7=AS|last8=Schuijers|first8=J|last9=Lee|first9=TI|last10=Zhao|first10=K|last11=Young|first11=RA|title=Control of cell identity genes occurs in insulated neighborhoods in mammalian chromosomes.|journal=Cell|date=9 October 2014|volume=159|issue=2|pages=374–87|pmid=25303531|doi=10.1016/j.cell.2014.09.030|pmc=4197132}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Ji|first1=X|last2=Dadon|first2=DB|last3=Powell|first3=BE|last4=Fan|first4=ZP|last5=Borges-Rivera|first5=D|last6=Shachar|first6=S|last7=Weintraub|first7=AS|last8=Hnisz|first8=D|last9=Pegoraro|first9=G|last10=Lee|first10=TI|last11=Misteli|first11=T|last12=Jaenisch|first12=R|last13=Young|first13=RA|title=3D Chromosome Regulatory Landscape of Human Pluripotent Cells.|journal=Cell Stem Cell|date=4 February 2016|volume=18|issue=2|pages=262–75|pmid=26686465|doi=10.1016/j.stem.2015.11.007|pmc=4848748}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Hnisz|first1=D|last2=Day|first2=DS|last3=Young|first3=RA|year=2016|title=Insulated neighborhoods: structural and functional units of mammalian gene control|journal=Cell|volume=167|issue=5|pages=1188–1200|doi=10.1016/j.cell.2016.10.024|pmc=5125522|pmid=27863240}}</ref> He has further shown that disruption of these neighborhoods in disease contributes to gene dysregulation.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Hnisz|first1=D|last2=Weintraub|first2=AS|last3=Day|first3=DS|last4=Valton|first4=AL|last5=Bak|first5=RO|last6=Li|first6=CH|last7=Goldmann|first7=J|last8=Lajoie|first8=BR|last9=Fan|first9=ZP|last10=Sigova|first10=AA|last11=Reddy|first11=J|last12=Borges-Rivera|first12=D|last13=Lee|first13=TI|last14=Jaenisch|first14=R|last15=Porteus|first15=MH|last16=Dekker|first16=J|last17=Young|first17=RA|title=Activation of proto-oncogenes by disruption of chromosome neighborhoods.|journal=Science|date=25 March 2016|volume=351|issue=6280|pages=1454–8|pmid=26940867|doi=10.1126/science.aad9024|pmc=4884612|bibcode=2016Sci...351.1454H}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2017/01/09/science/dna-tads.html?|title=A Family's Shared Defect Sheds Light on the Human Genome|last=Angier|first=Natalie|newspaper=The New York Times|date=January 9, 2017}}</ref>

Young and his colleagues have proposed that regulation of genes occurs in nuclear bodies called [[biomolecular condensate]]s.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hnisz |first1=D |last2=Shrinivas |first2=K |last3=Young |first3=RA |last4=Chakraborty |first4=AK |last5=Sharp |first5=PA |title=A Phase Separation Model for Transcriptional Control |journal=Cell |date=23 March 2017 |volume=169 |issue=1 |pages=13–23 |doi=10.1016/j.cell.2017.02.007 |pmid=28340338 |pmc=5432200}}</ref> These condensates compartmentalize and concentrate the diverse biomolecules needed for proper regulation of gene expression.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Boija |first1=A |last2=Klein |first2=IA |last3=Sabari |first3=BR |last4=Dall'Agnese |first4=A |last5=Coffey |first5=EL |last6=Zamudio |first6=AV |last7=Li |first7=CH |last8=Shrinivas |first8=K |last9=Manteiga |first9=JC |last10=Hannett |first10=NM |last11=Abraham |first11=BJ |last12=Afeyan |first12=LK |last13=Guo |first13=YE |last14=Rimel |first14=JK |last15=Fant |first15=CB |last16=Schuijers |first16=J |last17=Lee |first17=TI |last18=Taatjes |first18=DJ |last19=Young |first19=RA |title=Transcription factors activate genes through the phase separation capacity of their activation domains |journal=Cell |date=13 December 2018 |volume=175 |issue=7 |pages=1842–1855 |doi=10.1016/j.cell.2018.10.042 |pmid=30449618 |pmc=6295254}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Sabari |first1=BR |last2=Dall'Agnese |first2=A |last3=Boija |first3=A |last4=Klein |first4=IA |last5=Coffey |first5=EL |last6=Shrinivas |first6=K |last7=Abraham |first7=BJ |last8=Hannett |first8=NM |last9=Zamudio |first9=AV |last10=Manteiga |first10=JC |last11=Li |first11=CH |last12=Day |first12=DS |last13=Schuijers |first13=J |last14=Vasile |first14=E |last15=Malik |first15=S |last16=Hnisz |first16=D |last17=Lee |first17=TI |last18=Cisse |first18=II |last19=Roeder |first19=RG |last20=Sharp |first20=PA |last21=Chakraborty |first21=AK |last22=Young |first22=RA |title=Coactivator condensation at super-enhancers links phase separation and gene control |journal=Science |date=27 July 2018 |volume=361 |issue=6400 |doi=10.1126/science.aar3958 |pmid=29930091 |pmc=6092193 |article-number=eaar3958}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Guo |first1=YE |last2=Manteiga |first2=JC |last3=Henninger |first3=JE |last4=Sabari |first4=BR |last5=Dall'Agnese |first5=A |last6=Hannnett |first6=NM |last7=Spille |first7=JH |last8=Afeyan |first8=LK |last9=Zamudio |first9=AV |last10=Shrinivas |first10=K |last11=Abraham |first11=BJ |last12=Boija |first12=A |last13=Decker |first13=TM |last14=Rimel |first14=JK |last15=Fant |first15=CB |last16=Lee |first16=TI |last17=Cisse |first17=II |last18=Sharp |first18=PA |last19=Taatjes |first19=DJ |last20=Young |first20=RA |title=Pol II phosphorylation regulates a switch between transcriptional and splicing condensates |journal=Nature |date=August 2019 |volume=572 |issue=7770 |pages=543–548 |doi=10.1038/s41586-019-1464-0 |pmid=31391587 |pmc=6706314|bibcode=2019Natur.572..543G }}</ref><ref name="pmid32895492">{{cite journal| author=Daneshvar K, Ardehali MB, Klein IA, Hsieh FK, Kratkiewicz AJ, Mahpour A | display-authors=etal| title=lncRNA DIGIT and BRD3 protein form phase-separated condensates to regulate endoderm differentiation. | journal=Nat Cell Biol | year= 2020 | volume= 22 | issue= 10 | pages= 1211–1222 | pmid=32895492 | doi=10.1038/s41556-020-0572-2 | pmc=8008247 }}</ref> Young recently discovered that cancer drugs are concentrated in cellular condensates and has proposed that this pharmacodynamic behavior contributes to optimal drug action.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Klein |first1=I.A. |last2=Boija |first2=A. |last3=Afeyan |first3=L.K. |last4=Hawken |first4=S.W. |last5=Fan |first5=M. |last6=Dall'Agnese |first6=A. |last7=Oksuz |first7=O. |last8=Henninger |first8=J.E. |last9=Shrinivas |first9=K. |last10=Sabari |first10=B.R. |last11=Sagi |first11=I. |last12=Clark |first12=V.E. |last13=Platt |first13=J.M. |last14=Kar |first14=M. |last15=McCall |first15=P.M. |last16=Zamudio |first16=A.V. |last17=Manteiga |first17=J.C. |last18=Coffey |first18=E.L. |last19=Li |first19=C.H. |last20=Hannett |first20=N.M. |last21=Guo |first21=Y.E. |last22=Decker |first22=T.M. |last23=Lee |first23=T.I. |last24=Zhang |first24=T. |last25=Weng |first25=J.K. |last26=Taatjes |first26=D.J. |last27=Chakraborty |first27=A. |last28=Sharp |first28=P.A. |last29=Chang |first29=Y.T. |last30=Hyman |first30=A.A. |last31=Gray |first31=N.S. |last32=Young |first32=R.A. |title=Partitioning of cancer therapeutics in nuclear condensates |journal=Science |date=2020 |volume=368 |issue=6497 |pages=1386–1392 |doi=10.1126/science.aaz4427 |pmid= 32554597|pmc=7735713 |bibcode=2020Sci...368.1386K }}</ref>

=== Other activities ===
Young is also an educator, entrepreneur and aviator.  He teaches three courses at MIT, "[https://biology.mit.edu/undergraduate/current-students/subject-offerings/covid-19-sars-cov-2-and-the-pandemic/ COVID-19, SARS-CoV-2 and the Pandemic]", "Cell Biology: Structure and Functions of the Nucleus" and "Topics of Mammalian Development and Genetics", and guest lectures at numerous universities and research institutes worldwide.<ref>{{Cite web|url = https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=uiIWW2vR2YM|title = CSHL Keynote: Dr Richard Young, Whitehead Institute for Biomedical Research Mass. Institute of Tech|date = Oct 31, 2013|publisher = Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url = https://videocast.nih.gov/Summary.asp?File=18183&bhcp=1|title = Roles for Transcriptional Super-Enhancers in Cell Identity and Disease|date = November 21, 2013|publisher = NIH Center for Information Technology}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url = https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=z7jNOeo4DNU|title = CSHL 2015 Symposium Interview Series with Richard Young|date = May 30, 2015|publisher = Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory}}</ref> Young has founded multiple companies in the biotechnology industry, including [https://www.syros.com/ Syros Pharmaceuticals, Inc.], [https://www.camp4tx.com/ CAMP4 Therapeutics], [https://omegatherapeutics.com/ Omega Therapeutics] and [https://dewpointx.com/ Dewpoint Therapeutics].  He holds a commercial pilot license and is a member of the [[Aircraft Owners and Pilots Association]].
